content.title: Comprehensive Identity and Direction Document
content: I want to be the type of person who…
Pleasure
• plays guitar for enjoyment and expression.

• • • • • • • shares intimacy and connection through sex. gets up on stage at open mic nights and shares my voice. sprinkles “movement snacks” into daily life, keeping my body alive and playful. spends 10–15 focused minutes each day with my wife and kids. values and enjoys time alone without guilt. watches shows with my wife as a shared ritual, not just background noise. makes time for guy time, friendships, and laughter.
Must

• • • • • • • • • calmly and consistently handles bank transfers. invests time in building and updating my portfolio. stays on top of insurance without last-minute stress. keeps home insurance sorted and up to date. books and completes my MOT on time. maintains my car before problems arise. goes to the dentist as part of caring for myself. sees the hygienist regularly to protect my health. connects with my wider family, not just my immediate circle.
Routine

• • • • • • • • • • • • • turns lights out by 22:30 to protect my energy. deep squats for 2 minutes each day to stay mobile. goes to the gym 2–3 times a week. takes care of my teeth morning and night with interdental sticks, mouthwash, and brushing. showers daily, morning or evening, as a reset. loads the dishwasher at night to start tomorrow clean. tidies the kids’ toys at the end of each day. resets the kitchen each night, counters clear and tidy. clears clutter spots like the dining room daily. keeps the staircase clear of clothes and clutter. takes out the bins without delay. sorts laundry by lights and darks before it piles up.

completes one full laundry cycle (wash → dry → put away) each time. empties bathroom bins and wipes sinks as part of daily care. keeps the kids’ room tidy and gathers their laundry daily. drinks enough water, refilling through the day. does a 10–15 minute daily movement snack.

• • • • • • • • swaps junk food for healthier snacks. spends 5–15 minutes tidying the bedroom so it feels calm. cooks dinner at home 2–3 nights each week. takes a 10-minute walk as a fallback, no matter what. hangs from a bar or board for 2 minutes daily. goes upside-down for 2 minutes a day. does a backbend/bridge for 2 minutes a day. skips rope most days for fun and fitness.
My Values
Health

I value building habits that lead to positive effects over time, even if they involve short-term effort or discomfort. I recognise that both helpful and harmful habits are formed through repeated action. I focus on actions that improve my physical and mental capabilities in the long run. Sayings of reinforcement: “One step normally leads to another” “fucking around makes babies?” “would this week on repeat make me weak?”
Kindness

I value treating others with respect and honesty. I understand that everyone has different needs, experiences, and viewpoints. I strive to see things from their perspective and offer support that is helpful and within my ability to give. Sayings of reinforcement: “to lend a helping hand i must ask and understand” “being nice is a vice, being kind is a grind”
Drive

I value pursuing ambitious goals, even when the journey is difficult and uncertain. I make decisions based on insights found through exploration. I face challenges with determination but stay flexible, always looking for the most effective path to long-term success. Sayings of reinforcement: “i do my best this time so i can do better next time” “Hard work is a fight, thoughtful work is a dance”
Creation

I value turning my ideas into reality and take responsibility for what I create. I create to add to my personal record of agency and growth. I believe that creating is essential for growth and living fully. Sayings of reinforcement: “your creations will put your ideas to shame” “An idea unsensed by senses is senseless”
Purpose
To create a stable foundation for our family and loved ones to live their values and fulfil their purpose and mission.
My Mission Statement

We are committed to creating a stable foundation for our family and loved ones, enabling them to live by their values and fulfil their purpose and mission. We believe in building habits that lead to long-term well-being, treating others with respect and honesty, pursuing ambitious goals with determination and flexibility, and turning ideas into reality. By 2030, We aim to be part of a self-sustaining remote first business that generates surplus capital for future investments, allowing us to prioritise our family and maintain control over our time and focus.
Issues

Numb discontent with existence; sense of flatness and lack of meaning. Low energy and effort to move; physical heaviness and lethargy. Neglect of self-care: inconsistent exercise, poor diet, minimal hygiene unless required. Smoking and junk food undermining health despite awareness of risks. Relationship strain from avoidance, cleaning, and disconnection.
Triggers

Evidence of not living the way I want: undone tasks, missed workouts, poor eating. Work and structure demands; stress from hours, lack of flexibility. Alcohol; heavy drinking occasions leading to suicidal thoughts/fantasies. Conflict or conversations with partner highlighting imbalance in effort.
Negative Loops

Avoidance brings short-term relief but creates long-term backlog and guilt → reinforces “I’m a failure” thinking. Food, smoking, or wall-punching as dopamine hits give temporary relief but reinforce unhealthy cycles.
Lack of proactive planning → last-minute reactions → stress and missed opportunities → self- doubt.
Aspirations

Embodied vitality: becoming someone who moves dynamically, daily. Stable routines: bedtime, exercise, healthier eating, hydration, consistent hygiene. Healthier coping menu: constructive dopamine hits instead of destructive ones. Family connection: being more present, proactive, and engaged.
Planning as a skill: repeatable systems of plan → act → review → adjust, making progress scalable and resilient.
Strengths 1. Clarity of Values

You have already articulated your values (Health, Kindness, Drive, Creation, Purpose) with reinforcement sayings. This shows a strong sense of self-awareness and a compass you can return to when decision-making feels muddy.
2. System-Building Mindset
Your instinct is to create processes (plan → act → review → adjust). This meta-skill means you can turn almost any habit into a repeatable system once you break through initial resistance.
3. Reflective Practice

You are logging, transcribing, and even feeding your therapy sessions back into tools like GPT. That level of reflection is rare — it means you can generate insights and spot patterns rather than staying stuck in reactive loops.
4. Willingness to Experiment
From bedtime routines to dopamine menus to micro-workouts, you’re not afraid to test and tinker. This playful, experimental stance means setbacks don’t have to derail you; they can become data.
5. Commitment to Family

Your mission and aspirations keep circling back to your wife and kids. Even when you feel guilt or avoidance, your underlying drive is to create stability and connection for them. That anchor gives purpose to the harder habit changes.
6. Openness About Struggles
You’ve shared candidly about avoidance, low mood, smoking, even dark thoughts. This openness is itself a strength — it makes denial less likely and allows you to confront issues directly.

I want to be the type of person who…
Pleasure
• plays guitar for enjoyment and expression.

• • • • • • • shares intimacy and connection through sex. gets up on stage at open mic nights and shares my voice. sprinkles “movement snacks” into daily life, keeping my body alive and playful. spends 10–15 focused minutes each day with my wife and kids. values and enjoys time alone without guilt. watches shows with my wife as a shared ritual, not just background noise. makes time for guy time, friendships, and laughter.
Must

• • • • • • • • • calmly and consistently handles bank transfers. invests time in building and updating my portfolio. stays on top of insurance without last-minute stress. keeps home insurance sorted and up to date. books and completes my MOT on time. maintains my car before problems arise. goes to the dentist as part of caring for myself. sees the hygienist regularly to protect my health. connects with my wider family, not just my immediate circle.
Routine

• • • • • • • • • • • • • turns lights out by 22:30 to protect my energy. deep squats for 2 minutes each day to stay mobile. goes to the gym 2–3 times a week. takes care of my teeth morning and night with interdental sticks, mouthwash, and brushing. showers daily, morning or evening, as a reset. loads the dishwasher at night to start tomorrow clean. tidies the kids’ toys at the end of each day. resets the kitchen each night, counters clear and tidy. clears clutter spots like the dining room daily. keeps the staircase clear of clothes and clutter. takes out the bins without delay. sorts laundry by lights and darks before it piles up.

completes one full laundry cycle (wash → dry → put away) each time. empties bathroom bins and wipes sinks as part of daily care. keeps the kids’ room tidy and gathers their laundry daily. drinks enough water, refilling through the day. does a 10–15 minute daily movement snack.

• • • • • • • • swaps junk food for healthier snacks. spends 5–15 minutes tidying the bedroom so it feels calm. cooks dinner at home 2–3 nights each week. takes a 10-minute walk as a fallback, no matter what. hangs from a bar or board for 2 minutes daily. goes upside-down for 2 minutes a day. does a backbend/bridge for 2 minutes a day. skips rope most days for fun and fitness.
My Values
Health

I value building habits that lead to positive effects over time, even if they involve short-term effort or discomfort. I recognise that both helpful and harmful habits are formed through repeated action. I focus on actions that improve my physical and mental capabilities in the long run. Sayings of reinforcement: “One step normally leads to another” “fucking around makes babies?” “would this week on repeat make me weak?”
Kindness

I value treating others with respect and honesty. I understand that everyone has different needs, experiences, and viewpoints. I strive to see things from their perspective and offer support that is helpful and within my ability to give. Sayings of reinforcement: “to lend a helping hand i must ask and understand” “being nice is a vice, being kind is a grind”
Drive

I value pursuing ambitious goals, even when the journey is difficult and uncertain. I make decisions based on insights found through exploration. I face challenges with determination but stay flexible, always looking for the most effective path to long-term success. Sayings of reinforcement: “i do my best this time so i can do better next time” “Hard work is a fight, thoughtful work is a dance”
Creation

I value turning my ideas into reality and take responsibility for what I create. I create to add to my personal record of agency and growth. I believe that creating is essential for growth and living fully. Sayings of reinforcement: “your creations will put your ideas to shame” “An idea unsensed by senses is senseless”
Purpose
To create a stable foundation for our family and loved ones to live their values and fulfil their purpose and mission.
My Mission Statement

We are committed to creating a stable foundation for our family and loved ones, enabling them to live by their values and fulfil their purpose and mission. We believe in building habits that lead to long-term well-being, treating others with respect and honesty, pursuing ambitious goals with determination and flexibility, and turning ideas into reality. By 2030, We aim to be part of a self-sustaining remote first business that generates surplus capital for future investments, allowing us to prioritise our family and maintain control over our time and focus.
Issues

Numb discontent with existence; sense of flatness and lack of meaning. Low energy and effort to move; physical heaviness and lethargy. Neglect of self-care: inconsistent exercise, poor diet, minimal hygiene unless required. Smoking and junk food undermining health despite awareness of risks. Relationship strain from avoidance, cleaning, and disconnection.
Triggers

Evidence of not living the way I want: undone tasks, missed workouts, poor eating. Work and structure demands; stress from hours, lack of flexibility. Alcohol; heavy drinking occasions leading to suicidal thoughts/fantasies. Conflict or conversations with partner highlighting imbalance in effort.
Negative Loops

Avoidance brings short-term relief but creates long-term backlog and guilt → reinforces “I’m a failure” thinking. Food, smoking, or wall-punching as dopamine hits give temporary relief but reinforce unhealthy cycles.
Lack of proactive planning → last-minute reactions → stress and missed opportunities → self- doubt.
Aspirations

Embodied vitality: becoming someone who moves dynamically, daily. Stable routines: bedtime, exercise, healthier eating, hydration, consistent hygiene. Healthier coping menu: constructive dopamine hits instead of destructive ones. Family connection: being more present, proactive, and engaged.
Planning as a skill: repeatable systems of plan → act → review → adjust, making progress scalable and resilient.

Evidence of not living the way I want: undone tasks, missed workouts, poor eating. Work and structure demands; stress from hours, lack of flexibility. Alcohol; heavy drinking occasions leading to suicidal thoughts/fantasies. Conflict or conversations with partner highlighting imbalance in effort.
Negative Loops

Avoidance brings short-term relief but creates long-term backlog and guilt → reinforces “I’m a failure” thinking. Food, smoking, or wall-punching as dopamine hits give temporary relief but reinforce unhealthy cycles.
Lack of proactive planning → last-minute reactions → stress and missed opportunities → self- doubt.
Aspirations

Embodied vitality: becoming someone who moves dynamically, daily. Stable routines: bedtime, exercise, healthier eating, hydration, consistent hygiene. Healthier coping menu: constructive dopamine hits instead of destructive ones. Family connection: being more present, proactive, and engaged.
Planning as a skill: repeatable systems of plan → act → review → adjust, making progress scalable and resilient.
Strengths 1. Clarity of Values

You have already articulated your values (Health, Kindness, Drive, Creation, Purpose) with reinforcement sayings. This shows a strong sense of self-awareness and a compass you can return to when decision-making feels muddy.

Your instinct is to create processes (plan → act → review → adjust). This meta-skill means you can turn almost any habit into a repeatable system once you break through initial resistance.

You are logging, transcribing, and even feeding your therapy sessions back into tools like GPT. That level of reflection is rare — it means you can generate insights and spot patterns rather than staying stuck in reactive loops.
4. Willingness to Experiment
From bedtime routines to dopamine menus to micro-workouts, you’re not afraid to test and tinker. This playful, experimental stance means setbacks don’t have to derail you; they can become data.
5. Commitment to Family

Your mission and aspirations keep circling back to your wife and kids. Even when you feel guilt or avoidance, your underlying drive is to create stability and connection for them. That anchor gives purpose to the harder habit changes.
6. Openness About Struggles
You’ve shared candidly about avoidance, low mood, smoking, even dark thoughts. This openness is itself a strength — it makes denial less likely and allows you to confront issues directly.
I want to be the type of person who…
Pleasure
• plays guitar for enjoyment and expression.

• • • • • • • shares intimacy and connection through sex. gets up on stage at open mic nights and shares my voice. sprinkles “movement snacks” into daily life, keeping my body alive and playful. spends 10–15 focused minutes each day with my wife and kids. values and enjoys time alone without guilt. watches shows with my wife as a shared ritual, not just background noise. makes time for guy time, friendships, and laughter.
Must

• • • • • • • • • calmly and consistently handles bank transfers. invests time in building and updating my portfolio. stays on top of insurance without last-minute stress. keeps home insurance sorted and up to date. books and completes my MOT on time. maintains my car before problems arise. goes to the dentist as part of caring for myself. sees the hygienist regularly to protect my health. connects with my wider family, not just my immediate circle.
Routine

• • • • • • • • • • • • • turns lights out by 22:30 to protect my energy. deep squats for 2 minutes each day to stay mobile. goes to the gym 2–3 times a week. takes care of my teeth morning and night with interdental sticks, mouthwash, and brushing. showers daily, morning or evening, as a reset. loads the dishwasher at night to start tomorrow clean. tidies the kids’ toys at the end of each day. resets the kitchen each night, counters clear and tidy. clears clutter spots like the dining room daily. keeps the staircase clear of clothes and clutter. takes out the bins without delay. sorts laundry by lights and darks before it piles up.

completes one full laundry cycle (wash → dry → put away) each time. empties bathroom bins and wipes sinks as part of daily care. keeps the kids’ room tidy and gathers their laundry daily. drinks enough water, refilling through the day. does a 10–15 minute daily movement snack.

• • • • • • • • swaps junk food for healthier snacks. spends 5–15 minutes tidying the bedroom so it feels calm. cooks dinner at home 2–3 nights each week. takes a 10-minute walk as a fallback, no matter what. hangs from a bar or board for 2 minutes daily. goes upside-down for 2 minutes a day. does a backbend/bridge for 2 minutes a day. skips rope most days for fun and fitness.
My Values
Health

I value building habits that lead to positive effects over time, even if they involve short-term effort or discomfort. I recognise that both helpful and harmful habits are formed through repeated action. I focus on actions that improve my physical and mental capabilities in the long run. Sayings of reinforcement: “One step normally leads to another” “fucking around makes babies?” “would this week on repeat make me weak?”
Kindness

I value treating others with respect and honesty. I understand that everyone has different needs, experiences, and viewpoints. I strive to see things from their perspective and offer support that is helpful and within my ability to give. Sayings of reinforcement: “to lend a helping hand i must ask and understand” “being nice is a vice, being kind is a grind”
Drive

I value pursuing ambitious goals, even when the journey is difficult and uncertain. I make decisions based on insights found through exploration. I face challenges with determination but stay flexible, always looking for the most effective path to long-term success. Sayings of reinforcement: “i do my best this time so i can do better next time” “Hard work is a fight, thoughtful work is a dance”
Creation

I value turning my ideas into reality and take responsibility for what I create. I create to add to my personal record of agency and growth. I believe that creating is essential for growth and living fully. Sayings of reinforcement: “your creations will put your ideas to shame” “An idea unsensed by senses is senseless”
Purpose
To create a stable foundation for our family and loved ones to live their values and fulfil their purpose and mission.
My Mission Statement

We are committed to creating a stable foundation for our family and loved ones, enabling them to live by their values and fulfil their purpose and mission. We believe in building habits that lead to long-term well-being, treating others with respect and honesty, pursuing ambitious goals with determination and flexibility, and turning ideas into reality. By 2030, We aim to be part of a self-sustaining remote first business that generates surplus capital for future investments, allowing us to prioritise our family and maintain control over our time and focus.
Issues, Triggers, Negative Loops, and Aspirations
Issues

Numb discontent with existence; sense of flatness and lack of meaning. Low energy and effort to move; physical heaviness and lethargy. Neglect of self-care: inconsistent exercise, poor diet, minimal hygiene unless required. Smoking and junk food undermining health despite awareness of risks. Relationship strain from avoidance, cleaning, and disconnection.
Triggers

Evidence of not living the way I want: undone tasks, missed workouts, poor eating. Work and structure demands; stress from hours, lack of flexibility. Alcohol; heavy drinking occasions leading to suicidal thoughts/fantasies. Conflict or conversations with partner highlighting imbalance in effort.
Negative Loops

Avoidance brings short-term relief but creates long-term backlog and guilt → reinforces “I’m a failure” thinking. Food, smoking, or wall-punching as dopamine hits give temporary relief but reinforce unhealthy cycles.
Lack of proactive planning → last-minute reactions → stress and missed opportunities → self- doubt.
Aspirations

Embodied vitality: becoming someone who moves dynamically, daily. Stable routines: bedtime, exercise, healthier eating, hydration, consistent hygiene. Healthier coping menu: constructive dopamine hits instead of destructive ones. Family connection: being more present, proactive, and engaged.
Planning as a skill: repeatable systems of plan → act → review → adjust, making progress scalable and resilient.
Strengths 1. Clarity of Values

You have already articulated your values (Health, Kindness, Drive, Creation, Purpose) with reinforcement sayings. This shows a strong sense of self-awareness and a compass you can return to when decision-making feels muddy.
2. System-Building Mindset
Your instinct is to create processes (plan → act → review → adjust). This meta-skill means you can turn almost any habit into a repeatable system once you break through initial resistance.
3. Reflective Practice

You are logging, transcribing, and even feeding your therapy sessions back into tools like GPT. That level of reflection is rare — it means you can generate insights and spot patterns rather than staying stuck in reactive loops.
4. Willingness to Experiment
From bedtime routines to dopamine menus to micro-workouts, you’re not afraid to test and tinker. This playful, experimental stance means setbacks don’t have to derail you; they can become data.
5. Commitment to Family

Your mission and aspirations keep circling back to your wife and kids. Even when you feel guilt or avoidance, your underlying drive is to create stability and connection for them. That anchor gives purpose to the harder habit changes.
6. Openness About Struggles
You’ve shared candidly about avoidance, low mood, smoking, even dark thoughts. This openness is itself a strength — it makes denial less likely and allows you to confront issues directly.
I want to be the type of person who…
Pleasure
• plays guitar for enjoyment and expression.

• • • • • • • shares intimacy and connection through sex. gets up on stage at open mic nights and shares my voice. sprinkles “movement snacks” into daily life, keeping my body alive and playful. spends 10–15 focused minutes each day with my wife and kids. values and enjoys time alone without guilt. watches shows with my wife as a shared ritual, not just background noise. makes time for guy time, friendships, and laughter.
Must

• • • • • • • • • calmly and consistently handles bank transfers. invests time in building and updating my portfolio. stays on top of insurance without last-minute stress. keeps home insurance sorted and up to date. books and completes my MOT on time. maintains my car before problems arise. goes to the dentist as part of caring for myself. sees the hygienist regularly to protect my health. connects with my wider family, not just my immediate circle.
Routine

• • • • • • • • • • • • • turns lights out by 22:30 to protect my energy. deep squats for 2 minutes each day to stay mobile. goes to the gym 2–3 times a week. takes care of my teeth morning and night with interdental sticks, mouthwash, and brushing. showers daily, morning or evening, as a reset. loads the dishwasher at night to start tomorrow clean. tidies the kids’ toys at the end of each day. resets the kitchen each night, counters clear and tidy. clears clutter spots like the dining room daily. keeps the staircase clear of clothes and clutter. takes out the bins without delay. sorts laundry by lights and darks before it piles up.

completes one full laundry cycle (wash → dry → put away) each time. empties bathroom bins and wipes sinks as part of daily care. keeps the kids’ room tidy and gathers their laundry daily. drinks enough water, refilling through the day. does a 10–15 minute daily movement snack.

• • • • • • • • swaps junk food for healthier snacks. spends 5–15 minutes tidying the bedroom so it feels calm. cooks dinner at home 2–3 nights each week. takes a 10-minute walk as a fallback, no matter what. hangs from a bar or board for 2 minutes daily. goes upside-down for 2 minutes a day. does a backbend/bridge for 2 minutes a day. skips rope most days for fun and fitness.
My Values
Health

I value building habits that lead to positive effects over time, even if they involve short-term effort or discomfort. I recognise that both helpful and harmful habits are formed through repeated action. I focus on actions that improve my physical and mental capabilities in the long run. Sayings of reinforcement: “One step normally leads to another” “fucking around makes babies?” “would this week on repeat make me weak?”
Kindness

I value treating others with respect and honesty. I understand that everyone has different needs, experiences, and viewpoints. I strive to see things from their perspective and offer support that is helpful and within my ability to give. Sayings of reinforcement: “to lend a helping hand i must ask and understand” “being nice is a vice, being kind is a grind”
Drive

I value pursuing ambitious goals, even when the journey is difficult and uncertain. I make decisions based on insights found through exploration. I face challenges with determination but stay flexible, always looking for the most effective path to long-term success. Sayings of reinforcement: “i do my best this time so i can do better next time” “Hard work is a fight, thoughtful work is a dance”
Creation

I value turning my ideas into reality and take responsibility for what I create. I create to add to my personal record of agency and growth. I believe that creating is essential for growth and living fully. Sayings of reinforcement: “your creations will put your ideas to shame” “An idea unsensed by senses is senseless”

• • • • • • • • swaps junk food for healthier snacks. spends 5–15 minutes tidying the bedroom so it feels calm. cooks dinner at home 2–3 nights each week. takes a 10-minute walk as a fallback, no matter what. hangs from a bar or board for 2 minutes daily. goes upside-down for 2 minutes a day. does a backbend/bridge for 2 minutes a day. skips rope most days for fun and fitness.
My Values
Health

I value building habits that lead to positive effects over time, even if they involve short-term effort or discomfort. I recognise that both helpful and harmful habits are formed through repeated action. I focus on actions that improve my physical and mental capabilities in the long run. Sayings of reinforcement: “One step normally leads to another” “fucking around makes babies?” “would this week on repeat make me weak?”
Kindness

I value treating others with respect and honesty. I understand that everyone has different needs, experiences, and viewpoints. I strive to see things from their perspective and offer support that is helpful and within my ability to give. Sayings of reinforcement: “to lend a helping hand i must ask and understand” “being nice is a vice, being kind is a grind”
Drive

I value pursuing ambitious goals, even when the journey is difficult and uncertain. I make decisions based on insights found through exploration. I face challenges with determination but stay flexible, always looking for the most effective path to long-term success. Sayings of reinforcement: “i do my best this time so i can do better next time” “Hard work is a fight, thoughtful work is a dance”
Creation

I value turning my ideas into reality and take responsibility for what I create. I create to add to my personal record of agency and growth. I believe that creating is essential for growth and living fully. Sayings of reinforcement: “your creations will put your ideas to shame” “An idea unsensed by senses is senseless”
Purpose
To create a stable foundation for our family and loved ones to live their values and fulfil their purpose and mission.
My Mission Statement

We are committed to creating a stable foundation for our family and loved ones, enabling them to live by their values and fulfil their purpose and mission. We believe in building habits that lead to long-term well-being, treating others with respect and honesty, pursuing ambitious goals with determination and flexibility, and turning ideas into reality. By 2030, We aim to be part of a self-sustaining remote first business that generates surplus capital for future investments, allowing us to prioritise our family and maintain control over our time and focus.
Issues, Triggers, Negative Loops, and Aspirations
Issues

Numb discontent with existence; sense of flatness and lack of meaning. Low energy and effort to move; physical heaviness and lethargy. Neglect of self-care: inconsistent exercise, poor diet, minimal hygiene unless required. Smoking and junk food undermining health despite awareness of risks. Relationship strain from avoidance, cleaning, and disconnection.
Triggers

Evidence of not living the way I want: undone tasks, missed workouts, poor eating. Work and structure demands; stress from hours, lack of flexibility. Alcohol; heavy drinking occasions leading to suicidal thoughts/fantasies. Conflict or conversations with partner highlighting imbalance in effort.
Negative Loops

Avoidance brings short-term relief but creates long-term backlog and guilt → reinforces “I’m a failure” thinking. Food, smoking, or wall-punching as dopamine hits give temporary relief but reinforce unhealthy cycles.
Lack of proactive planning → last-minute reactions → stress and missed opportunities → self- doubt.
Aspirations

Embodied vitality: becoming someone who moves dynamically, daily. Stable routines: bedtime, exercise, healthier eating, hydration, consistent hygiene. Healthier coping menu: constructive dopamine hits instead of destructive ones. Family connection: being more present, proactive, and engaged.
Planning as a skill: repeatable systems of plan → act → review → adjust, making progress scalable and resilient.
Strengths 1. Clarity of Values

You have already articulated your values (Health, Kindness, Drive, Creation, Purpose) with reinforcement sayings. This shows a strong sense of self-awareness and a compass you can return to when decision-making feels muddy.
2. System-Building Mindset
Your instinct is to create processes (plan → act → review → adjust). This meta-skill means you can turn almost any habit into a repeatable system once you break through initial resistance.
3. Reflective Practice

You are logging, transcribing, and even feeding your therapy sessions back into tools like GPT. That level of reflection is rare — it means you can generate insights and spot patterns rather than staying stuck in reactive loops.
4. Willingness to Experiment
From bedtime routines to dopamine menus to micro-workouts, you’re not afraid to test and tinker. This playful, experimental stance means setbacks don’t have to derail you; they can become data.
5. Commitment to Family

Your mission and aspirations keep circling back to your wife and kids. Even when you feel guilt or avoidance, your underlying drive is to create stability and connection for them. That anchor gives purpose to the harder habit changes.
6. Openness About Struggles
You’ve shared candidly about avoidance, low mood, smoking, even dark thoughts. This openness is itself a strength — it makes denial less likely and allows you to confront issues directly.

Your mission and aspirations keep circling back to your wife and kids. Even when you feel guilt or avoidance, your underlying drive is to create stability and connection for them. That anchor gives purpose to the harder habit changes.
6. Openness About Struggles
You’ve shared candidly about avoidance, low mood, smoking, even dark thoughts. This openness is itself a strength — it makes denial less likely and allows you to confront issues directly.

content.title: The Type of Person I Want to Be
content: Comprehensive Identity and Direction Document
I want to be the type of person who…
• plays guitar for enjoyment and expression.

• • • • • • • shares intimacy and connection through sex. gets up on stage at open mic nights and shares my voice. sprinkles “movement snacks” into daily life, keeping my body alive and playful. spends 10–15 focused minutes each day with my wife and kids. values and enjoys time alone without guilt. watches shows with my wife as a shared ritual, not just background noise. makes time for guy time, friendships, and laughter.

• • • • • • • • • • • • • turns lights out by 22:30 to protect my energy. deep squats for 2 minutes each day to stay mobile. goes to the gym 2–3 times a week. takes care of my teeth morning and night with interdental sticks, mouthwash, and brushing. showers daily, morning or evening, as a reset. loads the dishwasher at night to start tomorrow clean. tidies the kids’ toys at the end of each day. resets the kitchen each night, counters clear and tidy. clears clutter spots like the dining room daily. keeps the staircase clear of clothes and clutter. takes out the bins without delay. sorts laundry by lights and darks before it piles up.

completes one full laundry cycle (wash → dry → put away) each time. empties bathroom bins and wipes sinks as part of daily care. keeps the kids’ room tidy and gathers their laundry daily. drinks enough water, refilling through the day. does a 10–15 minute daily movement snack.
My Values

I value building habits that lead to positive effects over time, even if they involve short-term effort or discomfort. I recognise that both helpful and harmful habits are formed through repeated action. I focus on actions that improve my physical and mental capabilities in the long run. Sayings of reinforcement: “One step normally leads to another” “fucking around makes babies?” “would this week on repeat make me weak?”

I value pursuing ambitious goals, even when the journey is difficult and uncertain. I make decisions based on insights found through exploration. I face challenges with determination but stay flexible, always looking for the most effective path to long-term success. Sayings of reinforcement: “i do my best this time so i can do better next time” “Hard work is a fight, thoughtful work is a dance”

My Mission Statement
Issues, Triggers, Negative Loops, and Aspirations
Issues
Triggers

Evidence of not living the way I want: undone tasks, missed workouts, poor eating. Work and structure demands; stress from hours, lack of flexibility. Alcohol; heavy drinking occasions leading to suicidal thoughts/fantasies. Conflict or conversations with partner highlighting imbalance in effort.
Negative Loops
Lack of proactive planning → last-minute reactions → stress and missed opportunities → self- doubt.
Aspirations
Planning as a skill: repeatable systems of plan → act → review → adjust, making progress scalable and resilient.
Strengths 1. Clarity of Values
2. System-Building Mindset
Your instinct is to create processes (plan → act → review → adjust). This meta-skill means you can turn almost any habit into a repeatable system once you break through initial resistance.
3. Reflective Practice

You are logging, transcribing, and even feeding your therapy sessions back into tools like GPT. That level of reflection is rare — it means you can generate insights and spot patterns rather than staying stuck in reactive loops.
4. Willingness to Experiment
5. Commitment to Family
6. Openness About Struggles
You’ve shared candidly about avoidance, low mood, smoking, even dark thoughts. This openness is itself a strength — it makes denial less likely and allows you to confront issues directly.
Comprehensive Identity and Direction Document

I believe that creating is essential for growth and living fully. Sayings of reinforcement: “your creations will put your ideas to shame” “An idea unsensed by senses is senseless”
Purpose
To create a stable foundation for our family and loved ones to live their values and fulfil their purpose and mission.
My Mission Statement

We are committed to creating a stable foundation for our family and loved ones, enabling them to live by their values and fulfil their purpose and mission. We believe in building habits that lead to long-term well-being, treating others with respect and honesty, pursuing ambitious goals with determination and flexibility, and turning ideas into reality. By 2030, We aim to be part of a self-sustaining remote first business that generates surplus capital for future investments, allowing us to prioritise our family and maintain control over our time and focus.
Issues, Triggers, Negative Loops, and Aspirations
Issues

Numb discontent with existence; sense of flatness and lack of meaning. Low energy and effort to move; physical heaviness and lethargy. Neglect of self-care: inconsistent exercise, poor diet, minimal hygiene unless required. Smoking and junk food undermining health despite awareness of risks. Relationship strain from avoidance, cleaning, and disconnection.
Triggers

Evidence of not living the way I want: undone tasks, missed workouts, poor eating. Work and structure demands; stress from hours, lack of flexibility. Alcohol; heavy drinking occasions leading to suicidal thoughts/fantasies. Conflict or conversations with partner highlighting imbalance in effort.
Negative Loops

Avoidance brings short-term relief but creates long-term backlog and guilt → reinforces “I’m a failure” thinking. Food, smoking, or wall-punching as dopamine hits give temporary relief but reinforce unhealthy cycles.
Lack of proactive planning → last-minute reactions → stress and missed opportunities → self- doubt.
Aspirations

Embodied vitality: becoming someone who moves dynamically, daily. Stable routines: bedtime, exercise, healthier eating, hydration, consistent hygiene. Healthier coping menu: constructive dopamine hits instead of destructive ones. Family connection: being more present, proactive, and engaged.
Planning as a skill: repeatable systems of plan → act → review → adjust, making progress scalable and resilient.
Strengths 1. Clarity of Values

You have already articulated your values (Health, Kindness, Drive, Creation, Purpose) with reinforcement sayings. This shows a strong sense of self-awareness and a compass you can return to when decision-making feels muddy.
2. System-Building Mindset
Your instinct is to create processes (plan → act → review → adjust). This meta-skill means you can turn almost any habit into a repeatable system once you break through initial resistance.
3. Reflective Practice

You are logging, transcribing, and even feeding your therapy sessions back into tools like GPT. That level of reflection is rare — it means you can generate insights and spot patterns rather than staying stuck in reactive loops.
4. Willingness to Experiment
From bedtime routines to dopamine menus to micro-workouts, you’re not afraid to test and tinker. This playful, experimental stance means setbacks don’t have to derail you; they can become data.
5. Commitment to Family

Your mission and aspirations keep circling back to your wife and kids. Even when you feel guilt or avoidance, your underlying drive is to create stability and connection for them. That anchor gives purpose to the harder habit changes.
6. Openness About Struggles
You’ve shared candidly about avoidance, low mood, smoking, even dark thoughts. This openness is itself a strength — it makes denial less likely and allows you to confront issues directly.
Comprehensive Identity and Direction Document
I want to be the type of person who…
• plays guitar for enjoyment and expression.

• • • • • • • shares intimacy and connection through sex. gets up on stage at open mic nights and shares my voice. sprinkles “movement snacks” into daily life, keeping my body alive and playful. spends 10–15 focused minutes each day with my wife and kids. values and enjoys time alone without guilt. watches shows with my wife as a shared ritual, not just background noise. makes time for guy time, friendships, and laughter.
Must

• • • • • • • • • calmly and consistently handles bank transfers. invests time in building and updating my portfolio. stays on top of insurance without last-minute stress. keeps home insurance sorted and up to date. books and completes my MOT on time. maintains my car before problems arise. goes to the dentist as part of caring for myself. sees the hygienist regularly to protect my health. connects with my wider family, not just my immediate circle.
Routine

• • • • • • • • • • • • • turns lights out by 22:30 to protect my energy. deep squats for 2 minutes each day to stay mobile. goes to the gym 2–3 times a week. takes care of my teeth morning and night with interdental sticks, mouthwash, and brushing. showers daily, morning or evening, as a reset. loads the dishwasher at night to start tomorrow clean. tidies the kids’ toys at the end of each day. resets the kitchen each night, counters clear and tidy. clears clutter spots like the dining room daily. keeps the staircase clear of clothes and clutter. takes out the bins without delay. sorts laundry by lights and darks before it piles up.

completes one full laundry cycle (wash → dry → put away) each time. empties bathroom bins and wipes sinks as part of daily care. keeps the kids’ room tidy and gathers their laundry daily. drinks enough water, refilling through the day. does a 10–15 minute daily movement snack.

• • • • • • • • swaps junk food for healthier snacks. spends 5–15 minutes tidying the bedroom so it feels calm. cooks dinner at home 2–3 nights each week. takes a 10-minute walk as a fallback, no matter what. hangs from a bar or board for 2 minutes daily. goes upside-down for 2 minutes a day. does a backbend/bridge for 2 minutes a day. skips rope most days for fun and fitness.

Health

I value building habits that lead to positive effects over time, even if they involve short-term effort or discomfort. I recognise that both helpful and harmful habits are formed through repeated action. I focus on actions that improve my physical and mental capabilities in the long run. Sayings of reinforcement: “One step normally leads to another” “fucking around makes babies?” “would this week on repeat make me weak?”
Kindness

I value treating others with respect and honesty. I understand that everyone has different needs, experiences, and viewpoints. I strive to see things from their perspective and offer support that is helpful and within my ability to give. Sayings of reinforcement: “to lend a helping hand i must ask and understand” “being nice is a vice, being kind is a grind”
Drive

I value pursuing ambitious goals, even when the journey is difficult and uncertain. I make decisions based on insights found through exploration. I face challenges with determination but stay flexible, always looking for the most effective path to long-term success. Sayings of reinforcement: “i do my best this time so i can do better next time” “Hard work is a fight, thoughtful work is a dance”
Creation

completes one full laundry cycle (wash → dry → put away) each time. empties bathroom bins and wipes sinks as part of daily care. keeps the kids’ room tidy and gathers their laundry daily. drinks enough water, refilling through the day. does a 10–15 minute daily movement snack.

The Type of Person I Want to Be
I want to be the type of person who…
Pleasure
• plays guitar for enjoyment and expression.

• • • • • • • shares intimacy and connection through sex. gets up on stage at open mic nights and shares my voice. sprinkles “movement snacks” into daily life, keeping my body alive and playful. spends 10–15 focused minutes each day with my wife and kids. values and enjoys time alone without guilt. watches shows with my wife as a shared ritual, not just background noise. makes time for guy time, friendships, and laughter.
Must

• • • • • • • • • calmly and consistently handles bank transfers. invests time in building and updating my portfolio. stays on top of insurance without last-minute stress. keeps home insurance sorted and up to date. books and completes my MOT on time. maintains my car before problems arise. goes to the dentist as part of caring for myself. sees the hygienist regularly to protect my health. connects with my wider family, not just my immediate circle.

completes one full laundry cycle (wash → dry → put away) each time. empties bathroom bins and wipes sinks as part of daily care. keeps the kids’ room tidy and gathers their laundry daily. drinks enough water, refilling through the day. does a 10–15 minute daily movement snack.

• • • • • • • • swaps junk food for healthier snacks. spends 5–15 minutes tidying the bedroom so it feels calm. cooks dinner at home 2–3 nights each week. takes a 10-minute walk as a fallback, no matter what. hangs from a bar or board for 2 minutes daily. goes upside-down for 2 minutes a day. does a backbend/bridge for 2 minutes a day. skips rope most days for fun and fitness.
Health

I value building habits that lead to positive effects over time, even if they involve short-term effort or discomfort. I recognise that both helpful and harmful habits are formed through repeated action. I focus on actions that improve my physical and mental capabilities in the long run. Sayings of reinforcement: “One step normally leads to another” “fucking around makes babies?” “would this week on repeat make me weak?”
Kindness

I value treating others with respect and honesty. I understand that everyone has different needs, experiences, and viewpoints. I strive to see things from their perspective and offer support that is helpful and within my ability to give. Sayings of reinforcement: “to lend a helping hand i must ask and understand” “being nice is a vice, being kind is a grind”
Drive

I value pursuing ambitious goals, even when the journey is difficult and uncertain. I make decisions based on insights found through exploration. I face challenges with determination but stay flexible, always looking for the most effective path to long-term success. Sayings of reinforcement: “i do my best this time so i can do better next time” “Hard work is a fight, thoughtful work is a dance”
Creation

I value turning my ideas into reality and take responsibility for what I create. I create to add to my personal record of agency and growth. I believe that creating is essential for growth and living fully. Sayings of reinforcement: “your creations will put your ideas to shame” “An idea unsensed by senses is senseless”
Purpose
To create a stable foundation for our family and loved ones to live their values and fulfil their purpose and mission.
My Mission Statement

We are committed to creating a stable foundation for our family and loved ones, enabling them to live by their values and fulfil their purpose and mission. We believe in building habits that lead to long-term well-being, treating others with respect and honesty, pursuing ambitious goals with determination and flexibility, and turning ideas into reality. By 2030, We aim to be part of a self-sustaining remote first business that generates surplus capital for future investments, allowing us to prioritise our family and maintain control over our time and focus.
Issues, Triggers, Negative Loops, and Aspirations
Issues

Numb discontent with existence; sense of flatness and lack of meaning. Low energy and effort to move; physical heaviness and lethargy. Neglect of self-care: inconsistent exercise, poor diet, minimal hygiene unless required. Smoking and junk food undermining health despite awareness of risks. Relationship strain from avoidance, cleaning, and disconnection.
Triggers

Evidence of not living the way I want: undone tasks, missed workouts, poor eating. Work and structure demands; stress from hours, lack of flexibility. Alcohol; heavy drinking occasions leading to suicidal thoughts/fantasies. Conflict or conversations with partner highlighting imbalance in effort.
Negative Loops

Avoidance brings short-term relief but creates long-term backlog and guilt → reinforces “I’m a failure” thinking. Food, smoking, or wall-punching as dopamine hits give temporary relief but reinforce unhealthy cycles.
Lack of proactive planning → last-minute reactions → stress and missed opportunities → self- doubt.
Aspirations

Embodied vitality: becoming someone who moves dynamically, daily. Stable routines: bedtime, exercise, healthier eating, hydration, consistent hygiene. Healthier coping menu: constructive dopamine hits instead of destructive ones. Family connection: being more present, proactive, and engaged.
Planning as a skill: repeatable systems of plan → act → review → adjust, making progress scalable and resilient.
Strengths 1. Clarity of Values

You have already articulated your values (Health, Kindness, Drive, Creation, Purpose) with reinforcement sayings. This shows a strong sense of self-awareness and a compass you can return to when decision-making feels muddy.
2. System-Building Mindset
Your instinct is to create processes (plan → act → review → adjust). This meta-skill means you can turn almost any habit into a repeatable system once you break through initial resistance.
3. Reflective Practice

You are logging, transcribing, and even feeding your therapy sessions back into tools like GPT. That level of reflection is rare — it means you can generate insights and spot patterns rather than staying stuck in reactive loops.
4. Willingness to Experiment
From bedtime routines to dopamine menus to micro-workouts, you’re not afraid to test and tinker. This playful, experimental stance means setbacks don’t have to derail you; they can become data.
5. Commitment to Family

Your mission and aspirations keep circling back to your wife and kids. Even when you feel guilt or avoidance, your underlying drive is to create stability and connection for them. That anchor gives purpose to the harder habit changes.
6. Openness About Struggles
You’ve shared candidly about avoidance, low mood, smoking, even dark thoughts. This openness is itself a strength — it makes denial less likely and allows you to confront issues directly.

Relationship strain from avoidance, cleaning, and disconnection.
Triggers

Evidence of not living the way I want: undone tasks, missed workouts, poor eating. Work and structure demands; stress from hours, lack of flexibility. Alcohol; heavy drinking occasions leading to suicidal thoughts/fantasies. Conflict or conversations with partner highlighting imbalance in effort.
Negative Loops

Avoidance brings short-term relief but creates long-term backlog and guilt → reinforces “I’m a failure” thinking. Food, smoking, or wall-punching as dopamine hits give temporary relief but reinforce unhealthy cycles.
Lack of proactive planning → last-minute reactions → stress and missed opportunities → self- doubt.
Aspirations

Embodied vitality: becoming someone who moves dynamically, daily. Stable routines: bedtime, exercise, healthier eating, hydration, consistent hygiene. Healthier coping menu: constructive dopamine hits instead of destructive ones. Family connection: being more present, proactive, and engaged.
Planning as a skill: repeatable systems of plan → act → review → adjust, making progress scalable and resilient.
Strengths 1. Clarity of Values

You have already articulated your values (Health, Kindness, Drive, Creation, Purpose) with reinforcement sayings. This shows a strong sense of self-awareness and a compass you can return to when decision-making feels muddy.
2. System-Building Mindset
Your instinct is to create processes (plan → act → review → adjust). This meta-skill means you can turn almost any habit into a repeatable system once you break through initial resistance.
3. Reflective Practice

You are logging, transcribing, and even feeding your therapy sessions back into tools like GPT. That level of reflection is rare — it means you can generate insights and spot patterns rather than staying stuck in reactive loops.
4. Willingness to Experiment
From bedtime routines to dopamine menus to micro-workouts, you’re not afraid to test and tinker. This playful, experimental stance means setbacks don’t have to derail you; they can become data.
5. Commitment to Family

Your mission and aspirations keep circling back to your wife and kids. Even when you feel guilt or avoidance, your underlying drive is to create stability and connection for them. That anchor gives purpose to the harder habit changes.
6. Openness About Struggles
You’ve shared candidly about avoidance, low mood, smoking, even dark thoughts. This openness is itself a strength — it makes denial less likely and allows you to confront issues directly.

content.title: Creation
content: • • • • • • • • swaps junk food for healthier snacks. spends 5–15 minutes tidying the bedroom so it feels calm. cooks dinner at home 2–3 nights each week. takes a 10-minute walk as a fallback, no matter what. hangs from a bar or board for 2 minutes daily. goes upside-down for 2 minutes a day. does a backbend/bridge for 2 minutes a day. skips rope most days for fun and fitness.
My Values
Health

I value building habits that lead to positive effects over time, even if they involve short-term effort or discomfort. I recognise that both helpful and harmful habits are formed through repeated action. I focus on actions that improve my physical and mental capabilities in the long run. Sayings of reinforcement: “One step normally leads to another” “fucking around makes babies?” “would this week on repeat make me weak?”
Kindness

I value treating others with respect and honesty. I understand that everyone has different needs, experiences, and viewpoints. I strive to see things from their perspective and offer support that is helpful and within my ability to give. Sayings of reinforcement: “to lend a helping hand i must ask and understand” “being nice is a vice, being kind is a grind”
Drive

I value pursuing ambitious goals, even when the journey is difficult and uncertain. I make decisions based on insights found through exploration. I face challenges with determination but stay flexible, always looking for the most effective path to long-term success. Sayings of reinforcement: “i do my best this time so i can do better next time” “Hard work is a fight, thoughtful work is a dance”

I value turning my ideas into reality and take responsibility for what I create. I create to add to my personal record of agency and growth. I believe that creating is essential for growth and living fully. Sayings of reinforcement: “your creations will put your ideas to shame” “An idea unsensed by senses is senseless”

Purpose
To create a stable foundation for our family and loved ones to live their values and fulfil their purpose and mission.
My Mission Statement

We are committed to creating a stable foundation for our family and loved ones, enabling them to live by their values and fulfil their purpose and mission. We believe in building habits that lead to long-term well-being, treating others with respect and honesty, pursuing ambitious goals with determination and flexibility, and turning ideas into reality. By 2030, We aim to be part of a self-sustaining remote first business that generates surplus capital for future investments, allowing us to prioritise our family and maintain control over our time and focus.
Issues, Triggers, Negative Loops, and Aspirations
Issues

Numb discontent with existence; sense of flatness and lack of meaning. Low energy and effort to move; physical heaviness and lethargy. Neglect of self-care: inconsistent exercise, poor diet, minimal hygiene unless required. Smoking and junk food undermining health despite awareness of risks. Relationship strain from avoidance, cleaning, and disconnection.
Triggers

Evidence of not living the way I want: undone tasks, missed workouts, poor eating. Work and structure demands; stress from hours, lack of flexibility. Alcohol; heavy drinking occasions leading to suicidal thoughts/fantasies. Conflict or conversations with partner highlighting imbalance in effort.
Negative Loops

Avoidance brings short-term relief but creates long-term backlog and guilt → reinforces “I’m a failure” thinking. Food, smoking, or wall-punching as dopamine hits give temporary relief but reinforce unhealthy cycles.
Lack of proactive planning → last-minute reactions → stress and missed opportunities → self- doubt.
Aspirations

Embodied vitality: becoming someone who moves dynamically, daily. Stable routines: bedtime, exercise, healthier eating, hydration, consistent hygiene. Healthier coping menu: constructive dopamine hits instead of destructive ones. Family connection: being more present, proactive, and engaged.
Planning as a skill: repeatable systems of plan → act → review → adjust, making progress scalable and resilient.
Strengths 1. Clarity of Values

You have already articulated your values (Health, Kindness, Drive, Creation, Purpose) with reinforcement sayings. This shows a strong sense of self-awareness and a compass you can return to when decision-making feels muddy.
2. System-Building Mindset
Your instinct is to create processes (plan → act → review → adjust). This meta-skill means you can turn almost any habit into a repeatable system once you break through initial resistance.
3. Reflective Practice

You are logging, transcribing, and even feeding your therapy sessions back into tools like GPT. That level of reflection is rare — it means you can generate insights and spot patterns rather than staying stuck in reactive loops.
4. Willingness to Experiment
From bedtime routines to dopamine menus to micro-workouts, you’re not afraid to test and tinker. This playful, experimental stance means setbacks don’t have to derail you; they can become data.
5. Commitment to Family

Your mission and aspirations keep circling back to your wife and kids. Even when you feel guilt or avoidance, your underlying drive is to create stability and connection for them. That anchor gives purpose to the harder habit changes.
6. Openness About Struggles
You’ve shared candidly about avoidance, low mood, smoking, even dark thoughts. This openness is itself a strength — it makes denial less likely and allows you to confront issues directly.

We believe in building habits that lead to long-term well-being, treating others with respect and honesty, pursuing ambitious goals with determination and flexibility, and turning ideas into reality. By 2030, We aim to be part of a self-sustaining remote first business that generates surplus capital for future investments, allowing us to prioritise our family and maintain control over our time and focus.
Issues, Triggers, Negative Loops, and Aspirations
Issues

Numb discontent with existence; sense of flatness and lack of meaning. Low energy and effort to move; physical heaviness and lethargy. Neglect of self-care: inconsistent exercise, poor diet, minimal hygiene unless required. Smoking and junk food undermining health despite awareness of risks. Relationship strain from avoidance, cleaning, and disconnection.
Triggers

Evidence of not living the way I want: undone tasks, missed workouts, poor eating. Work and structure demands; stress from hours, lack of flexibility. Alcohol; heavy drinking occasions leading to suicidal thoughts/fantasies. Conflict or conversations with partner highlighting imbalance in effort.
Negative Loops

Avoidance brings short-term relief but creates long-term backlog and guilt → reinforces “I’m a failure” thinking. Food, smoking, or wall-punching as dopamine hits give temporary relief but reinforce unhealthy cycles.
Lack of proactive planning → last-minute reactions → stress and missed opportunities → self- doubt.
Aspirations

Embodied vitality: becoming someone who moves dynamically, daily. Stable routines: bedtime, exercise, healthier eating, hydration, consistent hygiene. Healthier coping menu: constructive dopamine hits instead of destructive ones. Family connection: being more present, proactive, and engaged.
Planning as a skill: repeatable systems of plan → act → review → adjust, making progress scalable and resilient.
Strengths 1. Clarity of Values

You have already articulated your values (Health, Kindness, Drive, Creation, Purpose) with reinforcement sayings. This shows a strong sense of self-awareness and a compass you can return to when decision-making feels muddy.
2. System-Building Mindset
Your instinct is to create processes (plan → act → review → adjust). This meta-skill means you can turn almost any habit into a repeatable system once you break through initial resistance.
3. Reflective Practice

You are logging, transcribing, and even feeding your therapy sessions back into tools like GPT. That level of reflection is rare — it means you can generate insights and spot patterns rather than staying stuck in reactive loops.
4. Willingness to Experiment
From bedtime routines to dopamine menus to micro-workouts, you’re not afraid to test and tinker. This playful, experimental stance means setbacks don’t have to derail you; they can become data.
5. Commitment to Family

Your mission and aspirations keep circling back to your wife and kids. Even when you feel guilt or avoidance, your underlying drive is to create stability and connection for them. That anchor gives purpose to the harder habit changes.
6. Openness About Struggles
You’ve shared candidly about avoidance, low mood, smoking, even dark thoughts. This openness is itself a strength — it makes denial less likely and allows you to confront issues directly.

Purpose
To create a stable foundation for our family and loved ones to live their values and fulfil their purpose and mission.
My Mission Statement

We are committed to creating a stable foundation for our family and loved ones, enabling them to live by their values and fulfil their purpose and mission. We believe in building habits that lead to long-term well-being, treating others with respect and honesty, pursuing ambitious goals with determination and flexibility, and turning ideas into reality. By 2030, We aim to be part of a self-sustaining remote first business that generates surplus capital for future investments, allowing us to prioritise our family and maintain control over our time and focus.
Issues, Triggers, Negative Loops, and Aspirations
Issues

Numb discontent with existence; sense of flatness and lack of meaning. Low energy and effort to move; physical heaviness and lethargy. Neglect of self-care: inconsistent exercise, poor diet, minimal hygiene unless required. Smoking and junk food undermining health despite awareness of risks. Relationship strain from avoidance, cleaning, and disconnection.
Triggers

Evidence of not living the way I want: undone tasks, missed workouts, poor eating. Work and structure demands; stress from hours, lack of flexibility. Alcohol; heavy drinking occasions leading to suicidal thoughts/fantasies. Conflict or conversations with partner highlighting imbalance in effort.
Negative Loops

Avoidance brings short-term relief but creates long-term backlog and guilt → reinforces “I’m a failure” thinking. Food, smoking, or wall-punching as dopamine hits give temporary relief but reinforce unhealthy cycles.
Lack of proactive planning → last-minute reactions → stress and missed opportunities → self- doubt.

Embodied vitality: becoming someone who moves dynamically, daily. Stable routines: bedtime, exercise, healthier eating, hydration, consistent hygiene. Healthier coping menu: constructive dopamine hits instead of destructive ones. Family connection: being more present, proactive, and engaged.
Planning as a skill: repeatable systems of plan → act → review → adjust, making progress scalable and resilient.
